agentFirewall.flowTitle: Agent actions pass through policy before data leaves the enterprise.
agentFirewall.scopeNote: What this protects: agent tool calls routed through the firewall proxy. It can block sensitive-data egress and dangerous side-effects — but only in an enforcing mode, and only for traffic that actually passes through the proxy. MCP is one supported transport, not the product boundary.

agentFirewall.intro: Agent Firewall governs agent tool calls: a digest-only decision log (no raw payloads/secrets) plus egress and side-effect policy. Configuring the policy requires the mcp:configure capability.

scoring.capabilitiesDesc: Built-in security tools that run on every scan:
scoring.reachabilityDesc: Filter out CVEs in code paths that are never called
scoring.scoreExplanation: Your security rating is calculated from {active} active risk vectors across {categories} categories. Vectors without data are excluded and weights redistributed.
scoring.licenseScanDesc: Flag GPL, AGPL, SSPL and other copyleft/risky licenses
scoring.observationOnly: observation only — does not affect rating
scoring.autoTriageDesc: Dedup, merge, and score noise reduction automatically
scoring.cvePenaltyNote: Known vulnerabilities (CVEs) apply a diminishing penalty to the health score. The formula caps the maximum penalty at 25 points to prevent a single severe CVE from zeroing the score.
scoring.dimensionsDesc: The raw health score is a weighted composite of four dimensions, each scored independently:

confidence.l0: L0 — inferred from a single static signal; not yet verified.
confidence.l1: L1 — corroborated by one independent source.
confidence.l2: L2 — multi-source or live-DAST verified — penalty applies at full weight.

scoring.dimComplexity: Cyclomatic complexity of functions. High complexity = harder to maintain and more bug-prone.
scoring.standardsDesc: Code scanning follows industry-standard vulnerability taxonomies:
scoring.subVectorSub: Component drill-down — observations, weight, and contribution to the overall score.
scoring.whyFloorDesc: Micro-changes in score (e.g. 735 → 736) are not statistically significant. Flooring ensures every visible score change maps to at least one real risk vector change — making score movements explainable and actionable.
scoring.dimCoverage: Documentation score, README quality, API doc coverage.
scoring.dimDeadCode: Unused exports, unreachable code, orphan files. Increases attack surface.
scoring.dimSecurity: Secrets leaked, SAST findings, taint flows, known CVEs.
scoring.formulaNote: Raw engine score (0–100) mapped to display range (250–900), floored to nearest 10 — eliminates false precision.
scoring.iacScanDesc: Security checks for Terraform, Kubernetes, Docker configs
scoring.ciGateDesc: Block PRs with critical vulnerabilities before merge
scoring.methodDesc: The overall score is a weighted average across active dimensions. Dimensions without scan data are automatically excluded — their weight is redistributed to the remaining dimensions. This ensures organizations aren’t penalized for dimensions they haven’t measured yet.
scoring.subtitle: How Warroom calculates health scores and security grades
scoring.gradeA: Minimal risk. Strong security posture, clean code, good documentation.
scoring.gradeB: Low risk. Minor issues present, generally well-maintained.
scoring.gradeC: Moderate risk. Notable security or quality gaps requiring attention.
scoring.gradeD: High risk. Significant vulnerabilities or technical debt.
scoring.title: Scoring Breakdown

encryptionTransit.title: Encryption in Transit
encryptionTransit.body: All traffic is TLS 1.2+ enforced. HSTS is set on all production endpoints.

secretRedaction.title: Secret Redaction
secretRedaction.body: Execution traces and evidence are redacted before persistence — API keys, tokens, cookies, JWTs, and PEM keys are masked.

authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.body: Firebase Auth handles identity. API access requires a valid Firebase ID token on every request.

encryptionRest.title: Encryption at Rest
encryptionRest.body: Database volumes and file storage are encrypted at rest using AES-256.

staticAnalysis.title: Static Analysis
staticAnalysis.body: CodeQL scans run on every pull request across all repos. Gitleaks performs secret scanning on full commit history.

auditLogging.title: Audit Logging
auditLogging.body: Every privileged action writes a hash-chained audit entry. The chain integrity is verifiable via GET /audit?verify=true.

supplyChain.title: Supply Chain
supplyChain.body: All dependencies are scanned with Grype on every release. Container images are scanned with Trivy. SLSA provenance is attached to every PyPI release.

rbac.title: Role-Based Access Control
rbac.body: Four roles (owner / admin / member / guest) with per-surface permission gates. Sensitive operations require admin.

disclosure.encryptPrefix: Please encrypt sensitive reports using our
disclosure.encryptSuffix: . Do not disclose vulnerabilities publicly until we have had a chance to address them.
disclosure.resolve: Resolve critical and high-severity issues within 30 days
disclosure.credit: Credit researchers who report valid vulnerabilities (with permission)
disclosure.update: Provide a status update within 7 days
disclosure.intro: If you discover a security vulnerability in any Flyto2 product, service, or infrastructure, please report it responsibly. We commit to:
disclosure.title: Vulnerability Disclosure

incident.remediate: Remediate — patch the root cause and verify fix
incident.contain: Contain — isolate affected systems within 1 hour of detection
incident.assess: Assess — determine scope and impact within 4 hours
incident.notify: Notify — inform affected customers within 72 hours as required under GDPR Art. 33/34
incident.report: Report — publish a post-mortem for incidents affecting multiple customers
incident.intro: We maintain an internal incident response runbook. In the event of a breach or significant security incident affecting customer data, we will:
incident.title: Incident Response

scope.outOfScope: Out of scope: third-party services we integrate with, social engineering attacks, denial-of-service, and attacks requiring physical access.
scope.intro: The following are in scope for responsible disclosure:
scope.title: Scope

security.subtitle: We build security tooling. That means our own security practices need to meet the same bar we hold our customers to.
security.title: Security at Flyto2

secretScanning.title: Secret Scanning
secretScanning.body: Flyto-indexer scans for accidentally committed secrets. The engine's redaction layer prevents any secret detected in execution output from reaching disk.

credentials.title: Credential Handling
credentials.body: We never store plaintext credentials. API keys are hashed (SHA-256) before persistence. Execution traces are redacted of tokens, cookies, and PEM keys before storage.

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Sharing
thirdParty.body: We do not sell customer data. We share data with sub-processors only to the extent necessary to provide the service (Cloud Run, Cloud SQL, Firebase Auth).

isolation.title: Data Isolation
isolation.body: Each organization's data is isolated at the database level using org_id scoping. Row-level security is enforced on every query.

retention.title: Evidence Retention
retention.body: Execution traces and scan evidence are retained for 90 days by default. Customers can configure shorter retention or request immediate deletion.

location.title: Data Location
location.body: Customer data is stored on Google Cloud Platform in the asia-east1 (Taiwan) region by default. Data does not leave this region without explicit customer consent.

control.secretRedaction: Secret redaction on all evidence before persistence
control.firebaseAuth: Firebase Auth with ID-token verification on every request
control.shaPinning: Dependency SHA pinning in all CI workflows
control.gitleaks: Gitleaks secret scanning on full commit history
control.codeql: CodeQL static analysis on all pull requests
control.grype: Grype CVE scanning on every release (SBOM-backed)
control.trivy: Trivy container image scanning (HIGH/CRITICAL, SARIF to GitHub)
control.rbac: Role-based access control (owner / admin / member / guest)
control.slsa: SLSA provenance attestation on all PyPI releases

privacy.subprocessorsIntro: We use the following sub-processors to deliver the Service:

accountInfo.label: Account information:
accountInfo.body: When you create an account we collect your name, email address, and authentication credentials (managed by Firebase Auth). If you sign in via GitHub or Google OAuth, we receive the profile information those providers share with us.

scanResults.label: Scan results:
scanResults.body: We store the output of security scans (findings, scores, metadata) associated with your organization. This data is retained for 90 days by default and is never shared with third parties.

usageData.label: Usage data:
usageData.body: We collect standard server logs (IP address, user agent, request path, response code, timestamp) to operate and improve the Service. We do not use third-party analytics SDKs.

repoData.label: Repository and code data:
repoData.body: When you connect a repository, we clone a temporary copy to perform security analysis. We do not retain source code beyond the duration of the scan unless you explicitly request persistent indexing.

privacy.contactPrefix: Questions about this policy should be directed to

storage.body: Customer data is stored on Google Cloud Platform in the asia-east1 (Taiwan) region. Data is encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES-256). We employ role-based access control and hash-chained audit logging for all privileged operations. Execution traces are redacted of secrets before persistence.

terms.permittedUseIntro: You may use the Service solely for lawful purposes and in accordance with these Terms. You agree not to:

warroom.reachHow: Taint analysis traces user-controlled values (sources like request.args, env vars, file reads) through your code to dangerous functions (sinks like os.system, eval, raw SQL). When a value reaches a sink without going through a sanitizer, it's an "unsanitized flow" — the most likely class of security bug. Click any flow below to see the source / sink expressions and call path.

warroom.title: AutoFix — Tier 1 / 2 / 3 pipeline
warroom.hint: Tier 1 = deterministic rules (hand-coded, stable). Tier 2 = AI-proposed patches gated by lint + test + flyto-runner closed-loop probe. Tier 3 = patterns mined from repeated AI fixes get promoted into Tier 1 with human review. Every patch passes 4 verify gates before any PR is opened.

check.deserializationDesc: Probe unsafe object handling. Target insecure deserialization and server-side template injection.
check.accessControlDesc: Identify unauthorized data access. Check cross-tenant data leakage, authorization bypass, IDOR (data exposure risk), and improper access control.
check.businessLogicDesc: AI-driven logic testing. Detect complex input validation errors and general logic flaws that static scanners cannot identify.
check.codeInjectionDesc: Detect if untrusted data executes system commands. Focus on Remote Code Execution (RCE) and OS command injection.
check.fileMisconfigDesc: Scan filesystem risks including Local File Inclusion (LFI), unrestricted file upload, directory listing, path control, and error leakage.
check.secretsCryptoDesc: Discover hardcoded credentials/passwords, weak encryption, JWT validation bypass, and sensitive data exposure.
check.llmInjectionDesc: Ensure AI integrations are tamper-proof. Detect prompt injection, jailbreak, and system context leakage attempts.
check.sqlInjectionDesc: Identify injection vulnerabilities in database queries. Covers SQL injection, NoSQL injection, XPath injection, and LDAP injection.
check.authSessionDesc: Verify login security. Check for brute force vulnerabilities, missing/improper authentication, and cookie integrity issues.
check.clientSideDesc: Protect browser security. Scan for XSS, CSRF, open redirects, and web cache poisoning attacks.
check.hardeningDesc: Identify missing or misconfigured defense controls including GraphQL hardening, CORS misconfiguration, TLS issues, and incorrect HTTP security headers.
check.ssrfDesc: Test if your server can be tricked into making requests to internal infrastructure or external systems.

threatIntel.ransomwareLede: Victim claims from ~80 ransomware-group leak sites (LockBit, Black Basta, Akira, 8base, ...). Mirrored hourly via ransomware.live.

threatIntel.iocLookupLede: Indicators of compromise — C2 / URL / IP / phishing / credential exposure.
threatIntel.sensorMapLede: Origin countries of malicious IPs + C2 indicators + malware-distribution URLs observed against this org\
threatIntel.iocNoSurface: No attack surface mapped yet. IoC lookups match indicators against your discovered domains and IPs — run domain discovery first to populate this view.
threatIntel.malwareEmpty: Catalog not populated yet. MITRE ATT&CK ingester runs weekly on the worker.
threatIntel.malwareLede: MITRE ATT&CK software catalog — malware families + dual-use tooling. Refreshed weekly.
threatIntel.ransomEmpty: No incidents loaded yet. ransomware.live worker loop runs hourly.

threatIntel.feedNoRun: No successful feed run recorded yet.

dast.sensitiveFilesCriticalDesc: Critical sensitive files are publicly reachable

check.notMailDomain: Not a mail domain (no MX) — SPF / DMARC / DKIM not applicable

dast.graphqlIntrospectDesc: GraphQL introspection reveals the entire API schema including all types, queries, and mutations.
dast.cookieNoEncryptDesc: A cookie is set without the 'secure' flag, meaning it can be intercepted over HTTP connections.
dast.cookieSubdomainDesc: Authentication cookies scoped to parent domain can be accessed by any subdomain, increasing attack surface.
dast.axfrVulnerableDesc: The DNS server permits unrestricted zone transfers, exposing the full DNS record set.
dast.sensitiveFilesDesc: These files are publicly accessible and may leak source code, configuration, or credentials.
dast.sessionStorageDesc: Session tokens stored in localStorage/sessionStorage have no protection against injected third-party scripts.
dast.apiDiscoveryEmpty: Run discovery to scan for API endpoints, documentation, and frameworks.
dast.jwtSelfSignedDesc: The server accepts JWT tokens with self-signed JWK keys, allowing attackers to impersonate legitimate users.
dast.noHstsPreloadDesc: The domain is not on the HSTS preload list, so the first visit can be downgraded to HTTP.
dast.poweredByLeakDesc: The server exposes the framework/runtime it uses, helping attackers identify specific vulnerabilities
dast.sessionCookieDesc: The session cookie is missing 'httpOnly' and 'secure' attributes, making it vulnerable to theft by third-party scripts.
dast.sourceCodeGitDesc: Git repository files are publicly accessible, potentially exposing source code and sensitive configuration.
dast.clickjackingDesc: Without X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors, attackers can embed your site in an iframe for clickjacking attacks.
dast.cookieNoHttpDesc: A cookie without HttpOnly can be accessed by JavaScript, enabling session theft via XSS.
dast.cookiePoisonDesc: URL parameters can set cookie values, which may be exploitable for session manipulation.
dast.cspInlineCssDesc: CSP allows inline CSS, which can aid social engineering attacks by injecting misleading visual elements.
dast.cspFallbackDesc: CSP does not define a fallback for a directive, causing the browser to treat that content type as unrestricted.
dast.cspInlineJsDesc: CSP is configured but allows inline JavaScript, which is a common XSS attack vector.
dast.dirBrowsingDesc: Directory listings are enabled, potentially revealing hidden scripts, backup files, and sensitive information.
dast.envExposedDesc: Environment files expose infrastructure credentials, API keys, or other sensitive configuration information.
dast.graphqlWarning: Introspection is enabled — this exposes your full schema to anyone. Disable in production.
dast.heartbleedDesc: OpenSSL Heartbeat Extension vulnerability allows remote attackers to read process memory, potentially disclosing sensitive information.
dast.hstsNotSetDesc: HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) forces browsers to use HTTPS only. Without it, users are vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks via protocol downgrade.
dast.jwtInvalidDesc: The server accepts JWT tokens with invalid signatures, allowing attackers to modify token payload without detection.
dast.serverLeakDesc: The server is exposing its software identity via the Server header. This helps attackers identify known vulnerabilities
dast.sessionStorage: Session token found in browser storage
dast.sriMissingDesc: External scripts/styles loaded without integrity attributes can be tampered with if the CDN is compromised.
dast.tabNabbingDesc: Links with target='_blank' without 'noopener noreferrer' allow the target page to take control of the opener.
dast.aiRunningHint: Hard rules score your attack surface. AI finds patterns humans miss.
dast.cspNotSetDesc: CSP is the first line of defense against XSS and data injection attacks. It controls which content sources are allowed to load and execute.
dast.deleteConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete "{name}"? All discovery data, subdomains, and scan results will be permanently removed.
dast.noApiFindings: No API documentation, GraphQL, or JS bundle endpoints detected.
dast.sslExpiryDesc: The TLS certificate is about to expire, which will break HTTPS connections and trigger browser warnings.
dast.httpOnlyDesc: The site responds on HTTP without redirecting to HTTPS, exposing traffic to interception and modification.
dast.linkRepoHint: Link a repository to filter API routes
dast.noDnssecDesc: Without DNSSEC, DNS responses can be spoofed, potentially redirecting users to malicious servers.
dast.openPortDesc: A service port is publicly accessible that should typically be behind a firewall. Database, cache, and remote access ports exposed to the internet are high-risk attack vectors.
dast.cspEvalDesc: CSP does not block eval(), setTimeout() and other functions that can execute unknown code, enabling code injection.
dast.jwtNoneDesc: The server accepts JWT tokens signed with the 'none' algorithm, bypassing signature verification entirely.
dast.jwtWeakDesc: The JWT token returned by your server uses a weak or known compromised secret, allowing attackers to forge tokens for unauthorized access.
dast.noDmarcDesc: Without DMARC, there is no policy to handle emails that fail SPF/DKIM checks, enabling phishing attacks using your domain.
dast.noPageSpeed: No PageSpeed data yet. Run discovery to test.
dast.noSniffDesc: Without 'nosniff', browsers may interpret files as a different MIME type, enabling XSS via MIME-type confusion.
dast.notVerified: Not verified yet — run discovery to test
dast.hstsNotSet: TLS not enforced with valid HSTS header
dast.psHintA11y: Missing alt text, contrast issues, or ARIA labels.
dast.psHintPerf: Slow loading. Optimize images, lazy-load, reduce JS bundle.
dast.serverLeak: Server leaks info via 'Server' HTTP header
dast.cspNotSet: Content Security Policy (CSP) header not set
dast.emptyDesc: Add your domains to discover exposed services, SSL status, DNS records, tech stacks, and potential vulnerabilities across your external perimeter.
dast.noCaaDesc: No CAA record restricts which certificate authorities may issue certificates for this domain.
dast.noSpfDesc: Without SPF (Sender Policy Framework), attackers can send emails appearing to come from your domain (email spoofing).
dast.psHintSEO: Missing meta tags, no sitemap, or poor mobile usability.
dast.psHintBP: Using deprecated APIs or insecure resources.
dast.subtitle: Discover and monitor your external attack surface across all domains

mcp.modeObserveSub: Watching and logging decisions only — NOT blocking anything yet.

mcp.modeShadowSub: Evaluating policy in shadow — recording what it WOULD block, but not blocking yet.

mcp.stepEndpoint: Point the proxy at the ingest endpoint
mcp.testRequires: Requires mcp:configure. Records one diagnostic READ event.
mcp.modeSoftSub: Blocks the highest-risk calls; warns on the rest.
mcp.stepKeyDesc: Use Settings > API Keys and choose the MCP Guardian scope. The browser never stores this key; the MCP proxy or direct ingest caller sends it as X-Flyto-API-Key.
mcp.policyLede: Move the guardian along the safety ramp. The seven floor safety rules always evaluate; the rollout mode controls whether a DENY actually blocks the agent.
mcp.scopeNote: What this protects: MCP tool calls routed through the guardian proxy. It can block sensitive-data egress and dangerous side-effects — but only in an enforcing mode, and only for traffic that actually passes through the proxy. It does not constrain what a model reasons about, nor any action an agent takes outside MCP.
mcp.proxyEnv: For flyto-security-mcp, set FLYTO_ENGINE_URL to this engine host and enable FLYTO_MCP_USE_INGEST=1 so decisions come from this backend policy.
mcp.simSafe: No calls that previously proceeded would be newly blocked — safe to enable.
mcp.stepKey: Create an org API key with mcp:invoke
mcp.intro: MCP Guardian governs agent tool-calls: a digest-only decision log (no raw payloads/secrets) plus the egress/tool-call policy. Configuring the policy requires the mcp:configure capability.
mcp.title: MCP Security

allOrNothing.title: All or Nothing
allOrNothing.desc: Most tools run everything at once. If something goes wrong halfway through 1,000 items, you don't find out until the damage is done.

noApproval.title: No Way to Pause for Approval
noApproval.desc: Sometimes a person needs to review and approve before the next step runs. Most tools can't pause and wait — they just keep going.

validation.title: Bad Data Slips Through
validation.desc: When data doesn't look right — wrong format, missing fields — most tools just keep going. You end up with bad results and no idea where it went wrong.

cloud.title: Your Data Goes to the Cloud
cloud.desc: Some data shouldn't leave your computer or your company's network. But most automation tools require an internet connection and store your data somewhere else.

problem.subtitle: Most automation tools have a fundamental problem: once you hit "run", you lose control.
problem.title: Problems Other Tools

step1.title: Check Before Running
step1.desc: Before each step runs, Flyto2 checks that the data looks right. If something is missing or wrong, it stops and tells you.

step2.title: Keep Data Consistent
step2.desc: Data moving between steps must match what's expected. No surprises, no silent failures.

step3.title: Verify the Results
step3.desc: After each step finishes, the results are checked. If anything looks off, you'll know right away.

step4.title: Save Progress Along the Way
step4.desc: Flyto2 saves progress as it goes. If something fails later, you can go back to the last save point instead of starting over.

validation.subtitle: Most automation failures aren't about broken logic — they're about bad data that nobody caught in time.
validation.title: Why Checking Data Should Be

approval.title: Human Approval Steps
approval.desc: Add approval steps anywhere in your workflow. The system pauses and waits for a person to confirm before moving on.

rollback.title: Go Back & Continue
rollback.desc: Made a mistake? Go back to the last good point, fix it, and continue from there. No need to start over.

builtin.title: Built-in Data Checking
builtin.desc: Flyto2 checks the data at every step. If something doesn't look right, it stops immediately and tells you what's wrong — before it causes problems.

offline.title: Works 100% Offline
offline.desc: Flyto2 runs entirely on your computer. No internet needed. Your data never leaves your machine.

audit.title: Full Record of Every Step
audit.desc: Every step is recorded with screenshots, logs, and timestamps. You can always go back and see exactly what happened.

pause.title: Pause Anytime
pause.desc: Run one item first. Check if it's correct. Then run the rest. You're always in control of when things move forward.

diff.offline: **Works offline**: Runs 100% locally on your computer. No cloud dependency. Same privacy and security on free and paid plans.
diff.record: **Record & Replay**: Show Flyto2 what to do once. It memorizes every click and keystroke, then replays at machine speed.
diff.nocode: **No coding required**: If you can click and type, you can use Flyto2. No scripts, no APIs, no Zapier.
diff.timing: **Split-second timing**: Execute at the exact moment needed — flash sales, reservations, limited slots. Batch 100 items in the time it takes to do 1.
diff.title: What makes Flyto2 different
diff.pause: **Pause, Verify, Resume**: Other tools only have "not running" and "finished." Flyto2 lets you pause mid-task, check the state, correct mistakes, and continue exactly where you stopped.
diff.noapi: **No API needed**: Works directly in the browser with any website — even sites that have no API or only allow one-at-a-time operations.

llms.description: Flyto2 is a no-code automation tool that records your browser actions and replays them at full speed. Unlike other automation tools, Flyto2 lets you pause mid-execution, verify what it's about to do, and resume — you always stay in control. It runs entirely on your computer with no cloud dependency, so your data never leaves your machine.
llms.summary: Browser automation at machine speed. Record once, replay forever. No coding, no API needed — works on any website. Pause, verify, and resume at any step.
llms.title: Flyto2
